Elastic demand (|E| > 1): quantity changes proportionally more than price. Inelastic demand (|E| < 1): quantity changes proportionally less than price. The demand curve slopes downward. **Supply**: The quantity of a good or service that producers are willing and able to offer at various prices, ceteris paribus. The law of supply: as price increases, quantity supplied increases (direct relationship). The supply curve slopes upward. **Equilibrium**: The point where the supply and demand curves intersect. At equilibrium price (P*), quantity demanded equals quantity supplied (Q*). The market naturally tends toward equilibrium. **Surplus (excess supply)**: Occurs when price is ABOVE equilibrium. Quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded. Producers cannot sell all their goods, so price tends to fall toward equilibrium. **Shortage (excess demand)**: Occurs when price is BELOW equilibrium. Quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied. Consumers cannot buy all they want, so price tends to rise toward equilibrium. **Price elasticity of demand**: A measure of how responsive quantity demanded is to a change in price. Elastic demand (|E| > 1): quantity changes proportionally more than price. Inelastic demand (|E| < 1): quantity changes proportionally less than price. Unit elastic (|E| = 1): proportional change is equal.

19:15:30 [fairlib.modules.agent.simple_agent] INFO: Observation: [1] A steep curve is not necessarily inelastic, and a flat curve is not necessarily elastic (it depends on the price-quantity level). 6. **Confusing a change in demand with a change in quantity demanded**: "Demand" refers to the entire schedule/curve. "Quantity demanded" is a specific point on the curve. Saying "demand changed" means the curve shifted. Saying "quantity demanded changed" means you moved along the curve. 7. **Applying supply and demand backward**: Students think a decrease in supply (shift left) means the price will decrease. Actually, a decrease in supply raises the price (less available at every price level). 8. **Ignoring ceteris paribus**: Students change multiple variables simultaneously when analyzing a shift, making the outcome ambiguous. Proper analysis changes one variable at a time. 9. **Thinking price floors cause shortages and price ceilings cause surpluses**: It is the opposite. Price floors (above equilibrium) cause surpluses. Price ceilings (below equilibrium) cause shortages. 10. **Conflating demand and want**: Students think demand is just about desire. Economic demand requires both willingness AND ability to pay at a given price. ## Diagnostic Questions - "Did the price of the good itself change, or did something else change?
